summary.intent: Audit all 15 specialized agnostic components against their specs to close Epic #227, while reorganizing the bloated monolithic test files into per-component modules for both spec conformance and proptest state machines.
summary.outcome: Completed the full audit and test reorganization: split both test blobs into 15 per-component modules each, added missing proptest coverage for Clipboard/ColorField/FileUpload/ColorSwatch/ContextualHelp, created the audit closure document, fixed a branch-coverage regression that dropped ars-components below its 90% threshold, and committed after passing cargo xci --profile fast.

repo: Category audit pattern is consistent across data-display, utility, and now specialized: per-component modules under tests/spec_conformance/<category>/ and tests/proptest_state_machines/<category>/, plus an audit doc under docs/implementation/<category>-category-audit.md.
Proptest state machine tests use #[ignore] and must be run with -- --ignored; the fast profile does not run them automatically.
cargo xtask spec info <component> validates that a component resolves in the spec registry; cargo xtask spec validate and cargo xtask spec compile-snippets are the two spec gates.
ars-components has a 90% branch coverage threshold enforced by cargo xci --profile fast via target/coverage/native-fast.lcov; the isolated gate is cargo xci --profile fast coverage-native.
GitHub project board item status is updated via gh project item-edit with explicit --field-id and --single-select-option-id flags; the project ID is PVT_kwHOAKgEE84BTlkR (project #2 for fogodev).
Branch naming convention for Codex/agent work is codex/<task-description>; the worktree starts detached so a branch must be explicitly created before editing files.
Test assertions using &&, ||, or matches! with guards each count as instrumented branches in LLVM coverage, so overly complex assertions in tests can artificially reduce branch coverage.
mod.rs aggregators for split test directories should contain only shared imports/helpers and mod <component>; declarations—no test logic.
cargo +nightly fmt is the project formatter; cargo insta test is used for snapshot gate; both are separate steps from cargo xci.
The post-implementation audit skill lives at .agents/skills/post-implementation-audit/SKILL.md and defines a three-phase check (spec drift, missing coverage, test/contract gaps).

code.finding: Was a 343-line monolithic blob covering all 15 specialized components; replaced by specialized/mod.rs + 15 per-component files.
Was a 1018-line monolithic blob; replaced by specialized/mod.rs + 15 per-component files (16 ignored tests preserved, 5 new component modules added).

Missing unit coverage for public API dispatch, item subpart attrs, validation-message enum arms, ConnectApi dispatch, and builder clearers; these were the top lcov branch misses for the file_upload module.
Had uncovered branches in attr builders and sync logic identified by native-fast.lcov; fixed by adding targeted test cases for prop-sync OR-chain branches.
on_props_changed prop-sync matrix had several uncovered OR-chain branches; added a direct matrix test to cover aspect_ratio, min_zoom, max_zoom, and circular combinations.
Authoritative source for branch coverage; parsed with awk to extract per-file hit/total/miss counts; overall ars-components was 9507/10642 branches before fixes, 90.2% after.

workflow: cargo xci --profile fast runs in order: fmt → clippy → unit (workspace nextest + i18n splits) → integration → adapter → spec gates → native coverage; the coverage gate is the slowest and last.
Run cargo xci --profile fast coverage-native in isolation to validate the coverage threshold without re-running all earlier gates—saves significant time when iterating on coverage fixes.
Mechanical test splits should happen first (preserving all existing behavior) before adding audit-driven gap tests, so any failures are attributable to the split rather than new logic.
Use awk on the .lcov file to extract per-file miss counts before deciding what tests to add; avoids over-engineering tests for noise (debug impls, unreachable instrumentation artifacts).
cargo llvm-cov report --text --output-path /tmp/report.txt is needed when the inline coverage output is too large for the tool response buffer.
When a new test fails to compile due to an API mismatch (e.g., ComponentIds::from_id vs ::new), run cargo test --lib <module>:: in isolation before re-running the full fast profile.

friction: cargo xci --profile fast failed on the first full run because the audit work added test assertions with && and || that consumed branch budget, dropping ars-components from ~90.0% to just below the 90% threshold.
The coverage tool output was truncated/too large to return intact, requiring a workaround of writing to /tmp and parsing with awk to identify actionable gaps.
New proptest modules had compile errors: a non-existent color constructor, a borrowed temporary in attrs, and a contextual-help helper that returned an API tied to a local service scope.
ComponentIds API name mismatch (from_id vs new) was only caught at compile time during the focused lib test run, not during the initial patch application.
The full cargo xci --profile fast run takes long enough that it required multiple polling cycles (~10+ write_stdin waits) making it hard to know if the gate was stalled or just slow.
GitHub project item status update required knowing the exact --field-id and --single-select-option-id values ahead of time; these are not discoverable from gh issue view alone.
open_items: Pre-existing rustdoc warnings across unrelated categories in ars-components were not introduced by this PR but remain unresolved.
Mutation testing for specialized components is not a pre-PR gate; surviving mutants are deferred to Nightly-CI per repo policy.
Epic #227 is moved to In Progress but formal closure depends on the PR being merged; the GitHub issue close (Closes #227) is in the PR body, not yet executed.
Adapter implementations for the 15 specialized components remain out of scope for this audit and will need a separate epic/PR wave.
